model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The schedule-aware run improved slightly, which suggests optimization is moving in the right direction. The current flat MLP is still quite small for 256 fixed training steps, so a modest width increase is a simple next move that should improve representational capacity without changing training dynamics or adding much complexity.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The width increase helped, which points toward under-capacity more than optimization instability. Adding one more hidden layer is the next simple capacity increase for this flat MLP and should improve feature composition while keeping the training recipe unchanged.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Both prior layout changes improved loss, so capacity remains the clearest lever. Increasing the base width further is a simple follow-up that expands the earlier hidden layers while keeping the same depth, normalization, and optimization setup.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The last three successful changes all came from increasing model capacity, and the most recent width bump still improved loss. Pushing the base width one more step is the simplest way to test whether this MLP is still capacity-limited before shifting attention to optimizer tuning.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 7e-4","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Capacity scaling has produced steady gains, but the last width increase had a smaller return, which suggests optimization may now be the tighter bottleneck. A moderate learning-rate increase should help the scheduled Adam run use the fixed 256-step budget more aggressively without changing model structure or adding complexity.","secondary_modes":["layout","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 7e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The higher learning rate was harmful, so the next cleaner optimizer test is to keep the previously better scale and switch from Adam to AdamW. With the larger MLP now in place, decoupled weight decay may regularize the expanded capacity better without changing the training budget or scheduler shape.","secondary_modes":["topk","caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 3e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The wider, deeper AdamW model is now strong enough that regularization is a plausible next lever, and the optimizer switch only gave a marginal gain. A modest weight-decay increase is the simplest way to test whether the current capacity can generalize better within the same 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Recent optimizer and regularization tweaks have mostly plateaued, while this fixed-step regime still processes relatively few examples at batch size 64. Doubling the batch size is a simple way to expose the model to more data within the same 256-step budget and may improve stability for the larger AdamW MLP.","secondary_modes":["summaries","indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The batch-size increase produced by far the largest gain so far, which strongly suggests this fixed-step workload benefits from covering more training examples per optimizer step. Increasing to 256 is the simplest follow-up to test whether that effect continues before adding more coupled tuning changes.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The second batch-size increase produced another large gain, so the dominant signal is still that this fixed-step evaluator rewards processing more examples per update budget. Increasing to 512 is the most direct next test of whether that trend continues before coupling it with learning-rate retuning.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Three consecutive batch-size increases have produced the largest gains in this run, so the strongest current signal is still better sample coverage per fixed optimizer-step budget. Increasing to 1024 is the simplest next probe of that trend before introducing coupled learning-rate changes.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 2048","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The dominant empirical signal remains that larger batches substantially improve this fixed-step workload, and that trend has held through 1024. Increasing to 2048 is the simplest next test of whether more example coverage per optimizer step continues to outperform other tuning changes.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 4096","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 2048","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Larger batch sizes have been the clear dominant win throughout this run, and the gain from 2048 remains substantial. Increasing to 4096 is the simplest next step to keep exploiting the fixed-step budget by processing even more examples per update before attempting more coupled optimizer retuning.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 7e-4","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"Batch-size scaling is still helping, but the wall-clock cost is now rising sharply and the marginal gains are tapering, which makes it a good point to retune step size instead of only increasing batch further. With batch size 4096, a modest learning-rate increase is the simplest way to test whether the larger, more stable updates can use the fixed 256-step budget more effectively.","secondary_modes":["micro","indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 7e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The first learning-rate increase became helpful once the batch size was large, which suggests the large-batch regime still wants a more aggressive step size. Raising the learning rate to 1e-3 is the next simple scaling test and keeps the rest of the now-strong setup unchanged.","secondary_modes":["micro","indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The second learning-rate increase also improved validation loss, so the large-batch AdamW regime still appears underpowered at the current step size. Moving to 1.5e-3 is a modest continuation of that trend and is the simplest next test before touching the scheduler shape or other coupled settings.","secondary_modes":["micro","indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1.5e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The large-batch AdamW setup has improved across multiple successive learning-rate increases, and the latest bump was still beneficial. Raising the learning rate to 2e-3 is the next simple continuation of that scaling trend while keeping the rest of the tuned configuration fixed.","secondary_modes":["micro","indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    cosine = optim.lr_scheduler.CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=total_steps - warmup_steps, eta_min=LEARNING_RATE * 0.01)","old":"    cosine = optim.lr_scheduler.CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=total_steps - warmup_steps, eta_min=LEARNING_RATE * 0.1)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"Successive learning-rate increases are still helping, but the marginal gains are shrinking, which suggests the initial step size is closer to right while the late-training floor may now be too high. Lowering the cosine scheduler minimum from 10% to 1% of the peak learning rate is a simple way to keep the faster early optimization while allowing sharper refinement near the end of the fixed 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    cosine = optim.lr_scheduler.CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=total_steps - warmup_steps, eta_min=LEARNING_RATE * 0.1)","old":"    cosine = optim.lr_scheduler.CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=total_steps - warmup_steps, eta_min=LEARNING_RATE * 0.01)","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"Lowering the cosine floor hurt validation loss, so the late-training refinement was better with the higher minimum learning rate. Reverting the scheduler floor to 10% of the peak learning rate is the safest final step because it restores the best-performing schedule shape from the current run.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}
